However, the Table of Contents entry for \\u0026#34;Managing shared access signatures\\u0026#34; was left in place despite the corresponding section body being absent from the document.\\n\\n## Gaps Found\\n\\n- `sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/MigrationGuide.md` contains a Table of Contents link `[Managing shared access signatures](#managing-shared-access-signatures)` (line 19) that points to a section that does not exist in the document body. This creates a broken anchor link and misleads readers into expecting documentation for a deprecated feature.\\n- No mention anywhere in the guide that the KV-managed storage accounts / SAS feature has been deprecated, which could confuse users migrating from `Microsoft.Azure.KeyVault` who used that feature.\\n\\n\\n\\u003cstrong\\u003e📐 Implementation Guide\\u003c/strong\\u003e\\n\\nThis section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above.\\n\\n### Step 1: Modify files\\n\\n**File:** `sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/MigrationGuide.md`\\n**Action:** Edit\\n\\nTwo changes are needed in this file:\\n\\n**Change 1 — Remove the orphaned ToC entry** (around line 19):\\n\\nRemove this line from the Table of Contents:\\n```markdown\\n  - [Managing shared access signatures](#managing-shared-access-signatures)\\n```\\n\\n**Change 2 — Add a deprecation note for managed storage / SAS** after the \\u0026#34;Deleting secrets\\u0026#34; section and before the \\u0026#34;Additional samples\\u0026#34; section. Insert the following new section:\\n\\n```markdown\\n### Managing shared access signatures\\n\\nThe Azure Key Vault managed storage accounts feature — including SAS definition management — has been [deprecated]((learn.microsoft.com/redacted) Microsoft recommends storing SAS tokens or storage account keys directly as Key Vault secrets instead of using the managed storage accounts feature.\\n\\nIf you were previously using `KeyVaultClient` methods such as `SetSasDefinitionAsync`, `GetSasDefinitionAsync`, `GetSasDefinitionsAsync`, or `RegenerateStorageAccountKeyAsync` from `Microsoft.Azure.KeyVault`, you should migrate to managing your storage credentials manually and storing them as ordinary Key Vault secrets via `SecretClient.SetSecretAsync`.\\n\\nFor more information, see [Manage storage account keys with Key Vault and the Azure CLI (legacy)]((learn.microsoft.com/redacted)\\n```\\n\\n### Step 2: Add or update code snippets\\n\\nNo new snippet infrastructure is needed for this change — the new section is prose-only with no embedded code samples.\\n\\n### Step 3: Verify README structure\\n\\nNo README changes are required; this is a MigrationGuide-only fix.\\n\\nVerify `MigrationGuide.md` sections remain in correct order:\\n1. However, the Table of Contents entry for \\u0026#34;Managing shared access signatures\\u0026#34; was left in place despite the corresponding section body being absent from the document.\\n\\n## Gaps Found\\n\\n- `sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/MigrationGuide.md` contains a Table of Contents link `[Managing shared access signatures](#managing-shared-access-signatures)` (line 19) that points to a section that does not exist in the document body. This creates a broken anchor link and misleads readers into expecting documentation for a deprecated feature.\\n- No mention anywhere in the guide that the KV-managed storage accounts / SAS feature has been deprecated, which could confuse users migrating from `Microsoft.Azure.KeyVault` who used that feature.\\n\\n\\n\\u003cstrong\\u003e📐 Implementation Guide\\u003c/strong\\u003e\\n\\nThis section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above.\\n\\n### Step 1: Modify files\\n\\n**File:** `sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/MigrationGuide.md`\\n**Action:** Edit\\n\\nTwo changes are needed in this file:\\n\\n**Change 1 — Remove the orphaned ToC entry** (around line 19):\\n\\nRemove this line from the Table of Contents:\\n```markdown\\n  - [Managing shared access signatures](#managing-shared-access-signatures)\\n```\\n\\n**Change 2 — Add a deprecation note for managed storage / SAS** after the \\u0026#34;Deleting secrets\\u0026#34; section and before the \\u0026#34;Additional samples\\u0026#34; section. Insert the following new section:\\n\\n```markdown\\n### Managing shared access signatures\\n\\nThe Azure Key Vault managed storage accounts feature — including SAS definition management — has been [deprecated]((learn.microsoft.com/redacted) Microsoft recommends storing SAS tokens or storage account keys directly as Key Vault secrets instead of using the managed storage accounts feature.\\n\\nIf you were previously using `KeyVaultClient` methods such as `SetSasDefinitionAsync`, `GetSasDefinitionAsync`, `GetSasDefinitionsAsync`, or `RegenerateStorageAccountKeyAsync` from `Microsoft.Azure.KeyVault`, you should migrate to managing your storage credentials manually and storing them as ordinary Key Vault secrets via `SecretClient.SetSecretAsync`.\\n\\nFor more information, see [Manage storage account keys with Key Vault and the Azure CLI (legacy)]((learn.microsoft.com/redacted)\\n```\\n\\n### Step 2: Add or update code snippets\\n\\nNo new snippet infrastructure is needed for this change — the new section is prose-only with no embedded code samples.\\n\\n### Step 3: Verify README structure\\n\\nNo README changes are required; this is a MigrationGuide-only fix.\\n\\nVerify `MigrationGuide.md` sections remain in correct order:\\n1. Migration benefits ✅\\n2. General changes ✅\\n   - Package and namespaces\\n   - Separate clients\\n   - Client constructors\\n   - Setting secrets\\n   - Getting secrets\\n   - Listing secrets\\n   - Listing secret versions\\n   - Deleting secrets\\n   - **Managing shared access signatures** ← new prose section to add\\n3. Additional samples ✅\\n4. Support ✅\\n\\n### Step 4: Validate\\n\\nRun these commands in order:\\n\\n1. `dotnet build sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/`\\n2. `dotnet test sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/ --filter TestCategory!=Live`\\n3. `eng/scripts/Update-Snippets.ps1 keyvault` — confirm no README snippet regressions\\n4. `dotnet format sdk/keyvault/Azure.Security.KeyVault.Secrets/`\\n\\n\\n\\n## Next Steps\\n\\n\\u0026gt; [!TIP]\\n\\u0026gt; **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **`@copilot`** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above\\n\\n\\n\\n\\n\\u0026gt; Generated by [Update Docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/actions/runs/24157283885/agentic_workflow) · ● 556.8K · [◷](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3AAzure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net+is%3Aissue+%22gh-aw-workflow-call-id%3A+Azure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net%2Fupdate-samples-and-docs%22\\u0026amp;type=issues)\\n\\n\\n\\n\\n\",\"state\":\"open\",\"html_url\":\"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/57948\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"github-actions[bot]\",\"id\":41898282,\"profile_url\":\"https://github.com/apps/github-actions\",\"avatar_url\":\"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/15368?v=4\"},\"author_association\":\"NONE\",\"reactions\":{\"total_count\":0,\"+1\":0,\"-1\":0,\"laugh\":0,\"confused\":0,\"heart\":0,\"hooray\":0,\"rocket\":0,\"eyes\":0},\"created_at\":\"2026-04-08T20:51:54Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-08T20:51:54Z\"}","type":"text"}]}}}

support for JWE to Azure.Security.KeyVault 4.1+ or guidance on safely rolling keys\",\"body\":\"**Library or service name.**\\r\\nAzure.Security.KeyVault\\r\\n \\r\\n**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**\\r\\nKey Vault has features that make creating new versions of certificates and secrets easy.  However, it order to be able to safely expire a keys or certificate, you need to be able to keep track of which keys you have used to encrypt and sign data you already have.  From my research it would seem that JWE offers this ability.  It seems that all of the base components already exist in the Key Vault client libraries so it would be possible to create your own implementation, but this is an area where it would be easy to make a mistake, so having a dependable implementation would be beneficial.\\r\\n\\r\\nIf using JWE in this way is a terrible idea, then some documented guidance on how to deal with rolling keys would be a good
